author: @MrAntSmith
text:
I wonder if questions count as good or bad comments?
Was immnediately intrigued by the idea of polar printing but then wondered what happens about the non-linear resolution, since small angular steps give rise to larger arcs away from the axis than nearer to it... I guess its a matter of having a resolution greater than needed towards the center so the needed resolution can be delivered at the extent. Perhaps on an A4 bed that would mean around 20k steps/revolution, which is high but probably doable. But then perhaps it could scale in a hybrid arrangement, using a gross x/y mechanism to move a finescale, smaller polar-print head?
Anyway, just my immediate thoughts in case they are interesting...

author: @mfree80286
text:
More accurate and cheaper to build... hrmm... I had an idea a while back for a linear driver mechanism that I haven't built but still stew over in my mind occasionally. 

Premise is simple; long strands of material are available now that are extremely consistent in diameter.... extruded polymer strings. Like.... weed trimmer string. 
Attach a strand at the base of a rod attached to a stepper motor. Have a "rider" on the rod that has an angle on it that matches the wrap of the string around the rod, and spring load that rider so it keeps the string wrap tight. This will also need an @ shaped cover that keeps the string wrap from popping off the rod, and provides a track for the driven part to slide on. Oh, and a spool outside for excess string.

Operation? Rotation of the rod should move the rider by (degrees rotation/360)*(string diameter). In theory, a 1.5mm nylon string should move the rider 1.5mm for every 360 degrees of rotation. A 1.8 degree step should move the rider 0.0075mm.  Imagine what a finer version using piano wire could do.

Similar but limited, a roller on the surface of a ribbon being wound around a bobbin. This one takes a lot of room for large movements though.

Simplest yet is using a chinese windlass mechanism to pull an axis along against a strong spring. Run the wrap off the end of the differential spools to take up reels and you can get a decent range of motion, with enough wrap there's more than enough static friction to avoid slip thanks to the tension spring (or weight, for constant force, if you don't mind it hanging off the table)

author: @timmyssweet
text:
I’m a big supporter of your work, Robert.  On my Bambu H2D, I’m printing within 0.1 of a mm with ease.  Yes, I would like more accuracy if I could print jeweller, nuts and bolts, clock gears etc.  But, other materials and methods of manufacturing are suited to such tasks.  I don’t have them, so I am limited.  Perhaps, I’m spoiled to own a H2D as my first 3d-printer, and it’s perhaps it’s leaps ahead on accuracy than previous printers, so I have no idea what you’re talking about.  I don’t see the same problem that you see in terms of accuracy.  To me the layered print lines account for more variance than the position of the nozzle.  I’m puzzled. 

My advice is to double check your observation with the latest tech.  Is there enough variance in your 3d-prints that causes a problem?  And that raises the next question.  What are you doing Robert?  Do you, actually, what to make a windmill from plastic with plastic gears?  That will be interesting.  I can’t wait.  Keep us updated Robert.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good advice mate cheers

author: @TheEvertw
text:
The main trade-off in 3D printers is print speed vs accuracy.
Vibrations in the frame are one main cause of inaccuracies. But I guess the flex in the belt drives are another big one.
Key in reducing vibrations and flex is keeping the moving mass as low as possible yet keep the actuation rigid.

As a 3D printer has pretty constant configurations and predictable loads, it might be possible to use feed-forward control to dampen vibrations and flex in the mechanism at high speeds. Feed forward could be pre-calculated when calculating the G-code. You could even use the regular G-code from a slicer, then process it with a custom tool to apply the feed-forward and produce corrected G-code. The tool can read all the information it needs from the incoming G-code.

Altering the Marlin path generator to limit the jerk (the derivative of acceleration) might also be useful: high jerk is what excites vibrations. High jerk equals rapid changes in applied force. I know Marlin limits accelerations, but I don't know if it does the same with jerk—which is even more important to limit vibrations. Limiting acceleration reduced flex / bend / stretch, so that is useful as well. But it also limits the speed with which the head can move around.

However, calculating the path while limiting speed, acceleration and jerk is no joke, and you need high cycle speeds for it to be useful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @northwind7409
text:
Some control programs, whether CNC or 3D, I can't recall at the moment (maybe both), have backlash compensation settings, if you ever find a need for it. Let's face it, every connection in any mechanical arrangement can introduce a bit of slop, including bearings.

To improve traction in a friction drive, you might want to experiment with various coatings. I've used ordinary white glue to good effect, though not in your particular application. If you coat each surface with a thin, even coat, and then let them dry for several days before assembly, it might do what you want to prevent slippage. After that the dimensional compensation in your printer software can bring the end result closer to perfection. Just something more to play with.

author: @drashp
text: I had a manager whose mantra was don't highlight a problem if you don't have a solution because that's "negative". So wrong on so many levels. Seeing a problem may be the extent of someone's skill, but that could avert a costly mistake. Some see more, sometimes the mistake has to be highlighted to get the team to focus on it. Don't fear being negative.

replies.author: @smob0
replies.text:
Often times the solution to a problem is make sure things are safe and call an expert.

It is however, kind of the point of a manager to figure out these solutions. Bringing problems to their attention is also a solution. Like I don't know where to buy fire extinguishers or how many a store needs, but I do know if there isn't any fire suppression thats a problem.

replies.author: @chrisgill5692
replies.text: Yeah, I work in a safety critical industry.  No problem with highlighting problems or concerns, but it is super helpful if just the facts of the problem are presented, rather than pointless negativity.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Actually mate - problems and solutions go together. When you define a problem properly a solution presents itself . When you see a 'problem' and don't present a solution it is mostly just you expressing your fears  - not fears of the problem - fears of being thought of as presenting a 'wrong' solution.

replies.author: @drashp
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  Fair comment, that sums up engineering well - the art of solving problems via sound methodology. BUT..  sometimes the problem is the problem, and they (the "managers") don't want that solution.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Robert, are you going to build a 3D Printer yourself?

I purchased a printrbot kit when it was on the market years ago and while I did get it to work, it had issues that I don't have with the Q1 Pro which I think is due to my dusty apartment. 

One of the problems you're going to encounter with a more accurate printer, is the need for a finer filament output. This will require designing and making a heat end with a smaller hole, and filament output. So there will be an ultimate limitation as too how accurate a filament printer can be.

I don't know exactly how all that works out, but I have seen issues of clogging with those that have experimented with heat ends that have a smaller filament output.

Perhaps a hot end that is coated with your Graphene ink may be a solution to try.

author: @LivengoodScott
text: Your projects are a wonderful explanation to the workings of modern machines and their mechanics. I grew up on a farm in the 1960-70’s and we had so many powered machines to till, plant, spray, harvest, dry grain, move grain, grind feed, wash and grade eggs. I was lucky to have such a rich leaning environment. But what you do is explain these sometimes-esoteric mechanisms to city folks that did not interact with them. I really enjoy your work.  May you live long to keep doing what you enjoy.
